1.title: Transformer Superiority for Analog Robustness
1.finding: Transformers are 1.8x more robust than MLPs against voltage saturation, and up to 1.25x against weight noise.
1.method: Systematic comparison of AnalogTransformer vs AnalogMLP across 8 non-ideality configurations.
1.surprise: Residual connections HURT MLP robustness (-0.148 to -0.200), proving the attention mechanism is the driver.
1.significance: Changes the architectural paradigm for analog AI from MLPs to attention-based designs.

2.title: Curriculum Learning for Analog Robustness
2.finding: Progressive exposure to non-idealities during training recovers +4.8% analog accuracy on MNIST.
2.method: Cosine-scheduled spectral regularization + mismatch recycling + orthogonal initialization.
2.surprise: Standard training already achieves near-optimal analog robustness. Advanced methods help only at high non-ideality levels.
2.significance: Curriculum learning is dataset-dependent; most benefit on simpler datasets with high mismatch.

3.title: Analog Non-Ideality Hierarchy (Theorem 8)
3.finding: Additive perturbations (op-amp offset) propagate 5.2x more error through ReLU networks than multiplicative perturbations (mismatch).
3.method: Monte Carlo simulation of 5000 multi-layer ReLU networks comparing additive vs multiplicative error propagation.
3.surprise: Op-amp offset breaks at 0.05V (2.5% of supply) while mismatch survives to 50%. Offset is the #1 threat.
3.significance: Refocuses analog hardware design: prioritize offset cancellation over precision resistors.

4.title: The Analog Robustness Envelope
4.finding: Standard digital-then-deploy achieves 80% analog accuracy on MNIST at 20% mismatch with only 4-5% drop.
4.method: Stress test of 6 non-ideality parameters individually and combined at extreme levels.
4.surprise: The network is naturally robust to mismatch ≤ 20%. The combined stress shows a sharp phase transition at moderate levels.
4.significance: Analog hardware tolerances can be relaxed 20x (from 1% to 20% mismatch) without accuracy loss.

5.title: Energy Efficiency at 838x vs GPU
5.finding: Ultra-low-power analog achieves 838x energy efficiency over digital GPU at 65nm for 256x128 layers.
5.method: Technology-scaled energy model with power modes (standard/low/ultra_low), RC-limited clock, and node-specific pJ/MAC.
5.surprise: Old-node (65nm) analog outperforms state-of-the-art (7nm) digital, enabling cheap fabrication.
5.significance: Validates the core promise of analog AI: 100-1000x efficiency at low-cost nodes.

6.title: On-Chip Learning via Gradient-Free Optimization
6.finding: Hybrid SPSA achieves 44% analog accuracy on MNIST while NA-SPSA achieves 8.4% (random).
6.method: Zeroth-order SPSA, Noise-Aware SPSA, and Hybrid backprop+SPSA compared on 4 non-ideality levels.
6.surprise: Pure zeroth-order (SPSA/NA-SPSA) fails to converge in 20 epochs. Hybrid SPSA (backprop + SPSA) works.
6.significance: On-chip learning requires hybrid approaches. Pure gradient-free methods impractical for real hardware.

7.title: Temperature-Invariant Training
7.finding: Temperature-aware training (thermal noise augmentation, 0-70°C range) produces thermally robust networks with minimal accuracy loss.
7.method: Domain randomization over realistic temperature profiles (diurnal, step, random walk) during training.
7.surprise: TCR drift (< 1%) is negligible. Thermal noise (Johnson-Nyquist) is the dominant temperature effect.
7.significance: Automotive-grade (-40 to 125°C) analog AI is feasible with thermal noise augmentation during training.

8.title: Relaxed Design Rules for Analog AI
8.finding: Practical analog hardware tolerances: mismatch ≤ 20% (not 1%), offset ≤ 10mV, bits ≥ 4, rails ≥ 1.0V.
8.method: Systematic derivation from failure envelope empirical data validated on 2 datasets × 3 trials.
8.surprise: The industry standard of 1% resistor tolerance is 20x over-engineered for neural network inference.
8.significance: Enables low-cost analog fabrication: standard CMOS without precision components suffices.
